exemplar.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #5: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Clear focus on the prescribed title, with explicit definitions of key terms (âmodel,â âwrong,â âcorrectâ).
Effective use of examples from Natural Sciences (Bohrâs atom, phlogiston) and Mathematics (perfect circle) to link back to the central claim.
Some awareness of alternative viewpoints, such as recognizing that superseded models may retain pedagogical or practical value.
Relevant citations (e.g., Euclidâs postulate, Boxâs claim) demonstrating engagement with source material.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive; lines of reasoning are asserted rather than systematically developed.
Evaluation of counter-claims and alternative perspectives is brief and lacks depth or sustained critical engagement.
Several examples (e.g., âall models are useful,â mathematical model section) are under-developed or treated simplistically without nuanced justification.
The conclusion restates earlier points instead of synthesizing insights or reflecting on the essayâs broader epistemic implications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay meets the descriptors for the lower end of band 5â6: it maintains a clear, sustained focus on the title and links effectively to two areas of knowledge, with arguments supported by appropriate examples. However, analysis is uneven and largely descriptive, and evaluation of differing viewpoints lacks depth and critical nuance. On balance, the work demonstrates some coherent arguments and relevant linking but falls short of a more thorough, evaluative treatment required for higher bands. Consequently, a score of 5 accurately reflects its strengths in focus and example use, tempered by its limited analytical rigor and depth.

annotations.comment: The mathematical section is introduced but the example is not developed, ending abruptly; a concrete mathematical model illustration is needed to support the discussion.
The citation of Bertrand Russell is not linked back to Boxâs claim; the student should explain how axioms function as models and their epistemic status.
Invoking Euclidâs third postulate to illustrate the concept of a perfect circle model is a strong example of mathematical abstraction supporting the claim.
The essay neglects non-Euclidean geometries, which challenge the universality of the perfect circle model; alternative frameworks should be considered.
Linking the circle model to real-world applications such as wheel design effectively demonstrates the modelâs practical usefulness.
The assertion that âall models are usefulâ is unqualified; a clear set of criteria for usefulness and counter-examples of misleading models would strengthen the argument.
The reasoning risks circularity when using the utility of models to prove their universal usefulness; independent justification is required.
The student provides a clear, accessible definition of models and identifies mathematics and natural science as relevant areas of knowledge, establishing a coherent starting point for analysis.
The statement that mathematics has no wrong official models overlooks cases where conjectures or proofs have been overturned; this requires nuance.
The conclusion largely restates earlier arguments without synthesizing insights or evaluating the epistemic implications of Boxâs claim.
The distinction between âcorrectâ and âaccurateâ is asserted but not systematically justified; clearer operational definitions linked to examples are needed.
The essay moves directly from definition to argument without a clear roadmap; signposting the structure would improve coherence and reader guidance.
The use of the Bohr model effectively illustrates how a once-prevailing scientific model can remain pedagogically useful despite empirical limitations.
The analysis of the Bohr model remains largely descriptive; the student should critically evaluate how its supersession informs our understanding of model utility over time.
Reliance on tertiary sources like Britannica limits academic rigour; engaging with primary research or peer-reviewed studies would strengthen the argument.
The thesis statement is vague and does not specify the extent of agreement or disagreement with Boxâs claim; a more precise stance would strengthen focus.
Definitions focus solely on natural science; the student should introduce an equivalent definition for mathematical models here to ensure balanced coverage.
There is no explicit knowledge question guiding the analysis. Introducing a TOK question (e.g., on model revision and reliability) would sharpen the essayâs critical focus.
The introduction overlooks historical perspectives on modelling in TOK contexts; referencing thinkers such as Popper or Kuhn could deepen the conceptual framework.
Although phlogiston is a good counter-example, the student should link this to broader implications for the evolution of scientific knowledge and Boxâs claim.
There is no explicit linkage to the TOK areas of knowledge framework; the student should discuss methodological contrasts between natural science and mathematics here.
There is limited engagement with core TOK concepts (e.g., paradigm shifts, justification); integrating these would deepen the theoretical analysis.
Ethical implications of deploying flawed models (e.g., financial or epidemiological models) are not considered; discussing potential harms would add depth.
The inclusion of phlogiston theory as an example of a fundamentally wrong model shows awareness of how models can mislead scientific progress.
There is no acknowledgment of the essayâs own limitations or suggestions for further inquiry, which would strengthen critical self-reflection.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
